EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from a thunderstorm blew down several trees just southwest of Munson.

A weak tropical wave extending north from the west Caribbean Sea in combination with a mid/upper level trough of low pressure in the east Gulf of Mexico caused locally extreme rainfall over portions of the Broward-Miami-Dade metropolitan areas.  The most rain occurred in a swath from Hollywood to Pompano Beach with an official maximum of 10.21" measured at Fort Lauderdale-Hollywood International Airport.  Of that total, 7.36 inches fell from 6 to 8 PM which exceeds the 100-year maximum rate for that time period.  Radar estimates of 12 to 14 inches of rain were made near Oakland Park and Wilton Manors.According to Emergency Management estimates, 492 homes and businesses suffered some flood damage and many roadways were impassable.
Several trees were blown down between Bird Road and Miller Road.
A funnel cloud was sighted southwest of Belle Glade.
Power lines were blown down.
Thunderstorm winds damaged a carport, fence and metal shed, and blew down large tree limbs near Stuart.
Strong thunderstorm winds swept across the Deltona area blowing down dozens of trees that damaged houses and vehicles.
Thunderstorm winds blew down numerous trees and power lines in Deltona.
Severe thunderstorm winds broke off the tops of trees and damaged a carport in the Deer Run area.

Three people were swimming along an unguarded beach when they were caught in a rip current.  Two of the swimmers were able to make it safely ashore but the third one drowned.  The 15 year old males body was not found until two days later.
A local fire department reported a brief touchdown in an open field.
Golfball sized hail fell in the Walnut Hill area for the second time in about an hour.  Some roofs and vehicles sustained damage.
Large tree through roof of house on Lakefield Lane in Plainfield section of Orange Park. Live oak snapped in half on Dogwood Lane near Doctors Lake.  Source Clay Today 6/11/03.
Large trees and road signs down.
Thunderstorm winds blew down trees in the Longwood area.

A 41 year old male from Louisiana was swimming by himself in the gulf near Perdido Key.  The male got in trouble and someone was able to pull him out of the rough surf.   He was transported to a local hospital where he died the next day.
For the fourth time in a six hour time period, the Walnut Hill community experienced large hail as a result of severe thunderstorms.

A 34 year old male and a younger boy were swimming near Navarre Beach.  The rough water kept taking the two farther offshore and they began to struggle.  A surfer was able to paddle to them and brought the younger boy to shore.  By the time the rescuer got to the second individual he had already gone under.  The surfer was able to locate the individual and bring him ashore.  The individual was taken to a local hospital where he died.

Trained spotter watched the funnel cloud for 15 minutes and it breifly touched down in an open field once.  Law enforcement closed parts of Highway 60 for a short  time but the funnel remained south of the highway.
Doppler radar estimate of 4 inches of rain.  Standing water and pooling of water on secondary roads.
Tree limbs and roadside barricades blown down.
Lightning struck the home of a 72 year old woman and caused a fire that took fire fighters 15 minutes to put out.
High thunderstorm winds blew down power lines at Fort Walton Beach.
Northside of city.
Large trees down.
A funnel cloud was seen near the intersection of U.S. 441 and Lantana Road.
A tree was uprooted.
Large trees down on US Highway 17 south of Kingsley.
Large tree down on house off Plainfield Rd.  Power lines down in Doctors Inlet.  1600 persons without power overnight.
Heavy rainfall lead to standing and pooling of water in urban areas on Jacksonville Westside.
Large trees and power lines down on Adams Rd.  Pea sized hail reported in Wellborn. Report from Public Works Dept.

Three males were swimming in rough water near the Gulf Islands National Seashore.  They were pulled seaward by a strong current and got in trouble.  Rescuers were able to pull them ashore.  Two were taken to a local hospital where they were kept for observation overnight and were released the next day.
Washington Oaks State Park, reported by Park Service.
Washington Oaks State Park, reported by Park Service.  Large trees down in garden with power lines down outside park.
Trees uprooted and felled across power lines. 21,000 people affected by power outages.  Source Florida Times Union 6/7/03.
Large trees down.
Sighted on NW 83rd Strret in Gainesville.
Power Lines down on NW 83rd Street.  Roof removed from back porch on 1000 block of NE 21st street.  Second report from Gainesville Sun 6/10/03.
Lightning struck several transformers and knocked power lines down in Fort Walton Beach.
Emergency Management reported house windows broken and two cars slid off road due to hail.
Several large trees were downed by thunderstorm winds. One large oak tree fell into a parking lot and damaged three vehicles.
Gusty winds knocked down trees which took down power lines along Fern Road.
Local utility reported numerous trees and power lines down.
The Sheriff's Office reported roads closed due to high water. Water was deep enough to damage vehicles.
Golfball size hail reported on the east side of the city by the Madison County Sheriff.
Gusty winds and heavy rain downed large tree limbs at Lake Jovita near St Leo and power lines on River Road in Dade City.
Swells from Subtropical Storm Ana combined with an outgoing tide caused a small craft to capsize as it attempted to traverse the Jupiter Inlet.  Two tourists drowned and two others were rescued.
Thunderstorms winds ripped the roof off of one mobile home and the debris caused minor damage to nine other homes. .
A funnel cloud was reported near the intersection of Fruitvill and Mcintosh.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved south along the sea breeze front through the Citrus Springs and Hernando areas with multiple reports of one to two inch size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire chief reported nearly 20 minutes of hail which caused broken windshields and dented hoods to vehicles along with broken windows to some residences.
A trained spotter reported two inch hail while in his car at the intersection of Dale Mabry and Fern Road.

Lightning started a small fire in the kitchen of a home in the 900 block of South Nokomis Avenue Friday morning. The lightning hit the ground out back, ran through the water line to a PVC pipe in the wall and caught the insulation on fire.
A 14 year old male was standing next to a swimming pool when lightning struck nearby and injured him.  He was taken to a local hospital and kept for observation and released after a couple of hours.

Heavy rainfall fell across the south part of the county, causing several roads to be temporarily closed due to high waters.  Radar estimated 6 to 8 inches of rainfall fell over a three hour period.   The waters quickly drained after the rainfall ended, and the roads were reopened.
Storm shutters damaged and blown from a house.

Heavy rainfall during the past few days caused rapid rises on Munson Slough in southern Leon County which resulted in flooding of creeks, streams, drainage areas and a few homes.  Reported by the Florida DEM.
At least 28 people were rescued from rough surf off the Walton County beaches.  Six persons drowned.  Reported by the Walton County EMA.

Flash Flooding in the western third of Desoto County closed and damaged back roads. Several homes received water damage from rising waters. Rainfall of 11.79 occurred 10 miles west of Arcadia during the last three days.

A Pennsylvania woman was struck by lightning while collecting shells at Turtle Beach on the south end of Siesta Key. The bolt struck the woman near her neck, blackened her face, and left her swimming suit in shreds.  She was knocked unconscious and was in a coma for three days after the strike.  Her husband was performing CPR when the ambulance arrived.  He stated the storms and clouds were miles away and that this was a "bolt out of the blue".
A lightning strike near Airside A of the Tampa International Airport lead to one minor injury as an employee fell backwards and struck his head.
A lightning strike knocked out the power to Tropicana Field which delayed a major league baseball game for 18 minutes.

An eyewitness saw a lightning strike hit a palm tree and bounce into a home. The resulting fire heavily damaged one bedroom while the rest of the home received smoke damage. Two dogs in cages died of smoke inhalation.

The Manatee River at the Manatee Dam experienced a record crest of 42.42 fee on June 22nd. The flood stage is 41 feet. One of the three emergency flood gates on the dam malfunctioned and had to be forced open. About 700 homes within three miles of the Dam along the Manatee River evacuated and 230 people ended up at a Red Cross Shelter for 24 hours. One single family home received moderate flood damage and 12 homes received minor damage.

The Manatee River at Rye Bridge crested at 19.71 feet on June 22nd. Manual river readings were performed as the gauge went under water. This was the second highest crest ever and the highest crest since the Manatee Dam was built. The flood stage is 11 feet.

Additional heavy rains aggravated the flooding conditions in Manatee county. Heavy rains lead to four feet of water in several homes in Myakka City on Singletary Road. A daily rainfall of 6.21 inches was recorded 4 miles north northeast of Palmetto and 5.12 inches was recorded 2 miles southwest of Myakka City.
Heavy rains lead to street flooding and the flooding of several homes in Sarasota. Rainfall totals were 6.65 inches 4 miles west of Old Myakka and 4.34 inches 8 miles south of Edgeville.

One man was killed and another injured when they were struck by lightning while standing under a 30 foot oak tree. The injured man suffered from numbness to his right arm and leg and had burns on the bottom of his feet.

Numerous roads and homes were damaged in the Inglis and Yankeetown areas due to a flash flood. Rainfall totals for the day were 2.61 inches in Inglis and 2.35 inches in Williston. Further upstream of the Withlachoochee River the rainfall total was 4.65 inches near Dunnellon.

Heavy rain flooded 40 homes with 6-12 inches of water in the residential areas of Punta Gorda.  Also, 10,000 feet of a sea wall worth $2 million collapsed in Punta Gorda Isles and roads erosion closed two areas along County Road 74 near Westminster Road and Grove Blvd. Rainfall totals over the past three days totalled 9.49 inches in Point Lonesome and 8.35 inches 8 miles southeast of Punta Gorda.
Flash flooding closed three miles of Burnt Store Road from the Marina to Kismet Parkway and caused minor water damage to several businesses.
A RV Park in East Bonita Springs was evacuated as the area was inundated with four feet of water after heavy rains.
Thunderstorm wind gusts lifted a roof off an open hanger and broke the tie downs off of two planes.

The Myakka River at Myakka State Park recorded a record high crest of 12.46 feet on June 24th. The flood stage is 7 feet. One of the hardest hit areas was in the Hidden River neighborhood in far eastern Sarasota County. A 25 foot levy gave way causing $5.6 million in damage to 119 homes and $750,000 to roads and infrastructure. North Port received $1.3 million in damage with six homes destroyed, 21 homes with major damage, and 1318 homes or commercial structures impacted in the Estates area. In addition, 291 streets impassable for several days.
Several trees were downed by a brief thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Trees were uprooted and destroyed the park facility fence line on the south side of the Jacksonville Humane Society property.  The park was closed due to thunderstorm wind damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 21-year old male was seriously injured by lightning as he stood in his sister's backyard while visiting from New York.  The lightning struck the ground and flashed off of swing set to the victim.  The thunderstorm "had just  rolled in".
A man was injured by a lightning strike.
Dime-size hail was reported at the Sheriff's Office.
Several trees were blown down at an apartment complex at the 5500 block of Gunn Highway.
Several buildings sustained minor water damage and five parked cars were half under water in a parking lot on the University of South Florida campus.
Heavy rains and gusty winds downed a tree on two vehicles on the University of South Florida campus.
A utility company reported power lines down due to fallen trees and limbs from thunderstorm winds. Law enforcement reported two minor injuries due to falling trees at the Hillsborough State Park.

The Horse Creek near Arcadia recorded a record high crest of 18.02 feet on June 23rd. The flood stage is 12 feet. The nearby roads of Highway 72, County Road 769, and Pine Level Road were all closed as a result of the flooding.
The Peace River at Arcadia crested at 15.41 feet on June 26th. The flood stage is 11 feet.  A total of 11 homes were destroyed, two had major damage, and 60 others had minor damage.
Five separate funnel clouds were sighted from east of Naples to just offshore near the Isle of Capri.
A tornado briefly touched down near Sample Road and 2nd Way.

Roof ripped off apartment with subsequent rainfall flooding of structure.  Roof landed 30 feet away in back yard.  One female, age 65, transported to Shands Hospital, Jacksonville.  Source Florida Times Union, 6/29/03.
Palm trees down and flag pole bent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large trees were blown down and toppled power lines near A1A and Blackrock Road.
Quarter size hail was reported along Kernan Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Home totally involved when fire fighters arrived on scene.  House on Baden Powell Rd, West Putnam.  28 year old two story home valued at $150K, with $75K for contents.  Two family pets (cats) killed in fire.
Power lines down in northern portions of town.
Large trees down across northern portions of county.
Several large trees down at foot of Shands Bridge.
Two miles south of Kingley Ave, Orange Park.  Tree limbs broken and on ground.
Numerous trees down across city with largest at SE 18th Ave and on SW 85th St.

Thirty foot tree felled and went through roof of two homes in the Senior Citizens Village near Downtown Jacksonville. One room destroyed in one of the homes with unknown damage to the second. Source Florida Times Union 7/13/03.
Two power lines down with poles broken at south 15th street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Public reported seeing a funnel cloud while along Emerson Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement and the general public reported a funnel cloud over Lake Apopka.
Strong thunderstorm winds swept through a small community of mobile homes and older frame houses in the town of Oakland.  One house was seriously damage and 3 others received minor damage.
Local emergency management officials reported a funnel cloud over Basinger.
Thunderstorm winds damaged the roof of a dairy building north of Okeechobee.
After an estimated 8 to 12 inches of rain fell in a 7-hour period, Fisheating Creek rose above flood stage causing minor flooding of the Fisheating Creek Campground and inundating CR 720 near Muse.
St. Johns County Emergency Operations Center (EOC) communications tower struck by lightning knocking out some communications.  Information also obtained from St. Augustine Record.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nassau County Sheriff's Office and Nassau County 911 reported power lines down near Halliard about 1 mile south of County Road 108. The Sheriff's Office also reported several large trees down in Halliard at Carol Corner.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down in Palm Coast on Culvert Lane.
"A significant amount of hail."
Power lines down.
Described as penny sized hail.
Funnel dropped to within 200 feet of ground.  Strong rotation noted with surface winds estimated at 39 to 57 mph.   Near south end of Anastasia Island.
Heavy rain produced minor street flooding in Winter Garden.
Heavy rain produced rapid extensive flooding of major roadways in Melbourne.
The Cypress Creek at Worthington Gardens crested at 9.2 feet on June 27th and remained in flood into July. The flood stage is 8 feet.
The public reported a funnel cloud over Ocoee.

Twenty one roads were washed out and State Roads 62, 64, 70, and 72 were closed due to the flooding and flash flooding. Rainfall of 11.78 inches was recorded 4 miles northeast of Myakka Head during the past three days.
Fresh water flooding inundated areas for three days. Most of the damage was confined to the public infrastructure in the areas of Bowling Green, Wauchula, and Zolfo Springs.
The Peace River at Zolfo Springs Crested on June 25th at 17.71 feet. The flood stage is 16 feet.
An elderly man drowned in rough surf at the St. Joseph Peninsula State Park.  Reported by the Gulf County EMA.
Penny size hail reported by the public just east of the Tallahassee Regional Airport.
Large trees down in McIntosh and Fort McCoy.
Numerous trees down along U.S. Highway 441.  Roof of home partially blown off near Reddick.
Along highway 20 near Newnans Lake.
Trees reported as twisted off and downed at 3400 SW Williston Rd.  No report of a tornado.
Doppler radar estimate of 4 inches of rain mainly in northern portions of county.  Standing water and pooling of water on secondary roads.
An Osceola County official reported a funnel over East Lake Tohopekali.
The Volusia County Sheriff's office took two calls from the public reporting a funnel cloud near the Daytona Beach International Airport.

Victim was driving a 1997 Toyota 4Runner eastbound on Baya Lane, when a lightning bolt struck a large pine tree.  The tree fell on his vehicle and the victim was struck and killed by a branch which penetrated the front windshield.  Source Gainesville Sun 6/18/03.
Large tree down across power lines.
Two large trees down across power lines on Citrus Rd.
555 Seventh Ave. South, Jacksonville Beach.    Source Florida Union 6/18/03.

Touchdown near intersection of U.S. Highway 27 and 95th Street.  Minor damage to one barn and home at 9500 N. U.S. highway 27.  Screen pool cover destroyed at home and large pine tree down across barn damaging the roof.  Numerous trees and a fence line downed in area.  Additional information from Ocala Star Banner 6/23/03.
Large tree limbs (10 inch diameter) broken off.
7245 Crescent Oaks Court wires down.
Large limbs down on roadway 246 Sapelo Rd.
Numerous large trees and power lines down.
Large tree down on house.
Power lines blown down.
Large trees down on Highway 47 and Old Ichetucknee Rd.
Numerous large trees down on Highway 441.
Hail covered the backyard.  Several large limbs down.
Large trees and power lines down near Highways 41 and 129.
Mobile home set on fire by lightning strike.
Large trees and power lines down on County Road 232.  Power outages reported.
Reported as golfball sized hail.
Power lines down.
Large trees down.

19010 US 129 Mc Alpin.  One large pecan tree laid over onto two other pecan trees.  This snapped a second tree in half approximately six feet off the ground.  The second was snapped off two thirds of the way up with small branches three to four feet thrown approximately two hundred feet.  Three to four electrical poles snapped off with power out for eight hours.  Minimal damage to residential structure with screen panels blown out and canvas car port torn.

Korona...Trees down on Chicken Farm Rd.Flagler Beach...measured 40 knots gust.Bulow...Numerous trees down on mobile homes.  One tree punched a hole through one roof.  Numerous trees down on vehicles.  Some mobile homes had siding peeled off.

Several city blocks affected near Robin Hood Circle and Ratliff Rd. with a dozen large trees felled and bricks moved.  Privacy fence blown down.  Most trees were snapped off 8 to 14 inches above the ground, including a 10 inch round, healthy White Oak.  Four structures damaged, two were under construction.  Survey estimation, 200 yard wide by 400 yard long micoburst with wind speeds of 55 to 65 knots.  Broader wind swath of 40 knot winds. Additional information from Fernandina Beach News Leader 6/18/03
Large trees and power lines down.
Large trees and power lines down on Mockingbird Rd.
Several reports of large trees down across power lines just southwest of town.
Shed struck by lightning.
Roof ripped off shed.
Two trees down on a homes.
Large trees down across power lines west of the waterfront and near the old courthouse.  Power outages reported.
Near County Road 305 and State Road 13. Numerous reports of a funnel cloud along the St. Johns River near Racy Point.  One unconfirmed report of a touchdown; however, no damage reported.
Local utility reported numerous trees and power lines down in the western part of the county.  Some homes were without power for 24 hours.

Thunderstorms rolled in from the Gulf of Mexico Friday evening bringing damaging winds and rain that blew away vendors' tents at the Smooth Jazz Fest and left two inches of rain on the stage.  The winds also caused major damage to the roof of a police substation on South Greenwood Avenue as the communication tower was toppled.
A stationary thunderstorm blew the roof off a gabled secion of a roof-over of a mobile home
Numerous reports of trees and power lines down.  Most extensive damage just south of Jasper near US Highway 41 and Highway 129.  Pea sized hail also reported.
Estimated by Sheriffs Deputy.
Funnel cloud confirmed by St. Johns Sheriff's Office.
Lightning struck a utility pole next to Miles Elementary School and injured a 41 year old male 75 yards away. The victim appeared to be in shock and complained of numbness to his left arm and hand.

A small area of brief heavy rains of 4 to 6 inches in one hour caused a flash flood in Bradenton between 27th Street and 75th Street. An unidentified man, possibly homeless, misjudged the flooded 18th Avenue Drive West and rode his bicycle into the turbulent waters.  He was swept away into the Gulf of Mexico and his body was not found.  The water caused damage to 88 homes and flooded numerous cars in the parking lot of the DeSoto Square Mall. Twenty six single family homes and 20 multi-family homes received major damage.

Three to five inches of rain in 80 minutes caused one foot of water to enter some Sarasota businesses on 17th Street between Euclid and Mango Avenues. The listed damage amount does not include inventory. Numerous vehicles were abandoned in the area due to the high water.

The Little Manatee River near Wimauma crested at 15.18 feet at 12:15 AM EST on the 27th. Flood stage is 11 feet. Doppler radar detected a small area of 3 to 5 inches of rain over the head waters of the Little Manatee River from August 19th through the 20th. No significant damage was reported as these areas have been in and out of flood since June and the damage had already occurred.
A lightning-initiated fire destroyed a house in Southwest Ranches.  Ammunition in the house exploded.
Lightning struck the roof of a house causing a fire that completely destroyed the residence.
East winds around 15 mph created rip currents that drowned a Miami resident while swimming in the ocean near 41st Street on Miami Beach.

Strong onshore winds continued during the second day of the three-day Labor Day Weekend.   Two local residents swimming in the ocean late at night were caught in a rip current.  One of the women drowned.  On Saturday and Sunday, August 30 and 31, more than 100 persons were rescued from rip currents along the beaches of southeast Florida.
Lightning struck an apartment building and caused a fire in two rooms. Four families were displaced because of the fire and smoke damage.

A stationary thunderstorm dropped 5 to 7 inches of rain in one hour near the Brooksville Airport and caused rapid flooding of roads.  Emergency Management reported the water retreated as fast as it came, with the flood lasting only for 50-80 minutes.  No significant damage occurred due to the lack of homes in the area.  The thunderstorm developed on the north side of a tropical low as it moved onshore near Mediera Beach in Pinellas County. This tropical low was formerly known as Tropical Storm Henri that produced winds of 41 knots with gusts to 52 knots at Buoy 42036 100 NM west northwest of Bayport, FL on September 5th at 1200 EST.
A man in rural Lee County was struck by lightning, taken to the hospital, then released.

A nearly stationary thunderstorm formed along a sea breeze boundary across Cape Coral and dropped four to six inches of rain in three hours.  The excessive rain caused widespread flooding along Hancock Bridge Parkway from Sarabarbra Blvd to N.E. 24th Ave.
High Surf and long period swell from Hurricane Isabel with associated beach erosion.  Breakers of 7 to 10 feet with 2 to 4 feet of beach erosion reported.
High Surf and long period swell from Hurricane Isabel with associated beach erosion.  Breakers of 7 to 10 feet with 2 to 4 feet of beach erosion reported.

High astronomical tide combined with a strong northeast flow led to minor coastal flooding and beach erosion.  Effects were most pronounced in Flagler County but were felt from the Altamaha Sound, Georgia south to Flagler Beach, Florida.
Cypress Creek at Worthington Gardens went above flood stage on August 2nd, crested at 10.27 fee on August 13th, and fell below flood stage on September 21st.  Flood stage is 8 feet.
The Myakka River at Myakka State Park went above flood stage on August 5th, created at 10.02 feet on August 13th, and fell below flood stage on September 19th. Flood stage is 7 feet.
The Withlacooche River at Trilby went above flood stage on August 11th, created at 13.95 feet on August 24th, and fell below flood stage on September 13th.  Flood stage is 12 feet.

A feeder band ahead of Tropical Storm Henri produced seven inches of rain in three hours in the Tee-N-Green area of Punta Gorda.  Numerous streets were flooded and water lapped into homes due to the wakes produced by passing vehicles. The hardest hit area was Marion Street and the Whidden Industrial area in Punta Gorda, and along Edgewater and Kings Highway in Port Charlotte.
Three-inch diameter tree limbs were broken off.
A funnel cloud was spotted over the everglades.

Tourist drowned when caught in rip current at an unguarded beach.  Rough surf from Hurricane Isabel was developing and the Jacksonville surf forecast indicated a "moderate risk of rip currents" for the day with increasing surf later in the week. Nassau County Beach Patrol reported 6 rescues due to rip currents.

A total of 5 to 9 inches of rain fell during the event with the hardest hit area along Price and Salford Boulevards in North Port. The flooding was severe enough that the American Red Cross opened a shelter for displaced residents.
High Surf and long period swell from Hurricane Isabel.  Breakers of 7 to 10 feet reported with Nassau County beaches closed to swimming.
The Myakka River at Myakka State Park crested at 10.02 feet at 5:00 AM EST on the 13th. Flood stage is 7 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rains from August 7 to 11 combined with saturated soils created another round flooding across west central Florida.  No significant damage was reported as these areas have been in and out of flood since June and the damage had already occurred. Most other rivers across west central and southwest Florida reached at least one foot above flood stage during the month. Rainfall totals during the five day period included 10.90 inches 5 miles southeast of Bradenton, 6.51 inches at the Tampa International Airport, 6.27 inches in Venice, 5.62 inches near Holiday, and 4.67 in Brooksville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Withlacoochee River at Trilby crested at 13.95 feet at 3:00 AM EST on the 24th.  Flood stage is 12 feet.

High Surf and long period swell from Hurricane Isabel.  General breakers of 7 to 10 feet reported with occasional 12 to 15 foot sets.  Flagler Beach Pier was closed with boards broken off in the seaward section as waves broke over the end of the pier.  The pier was reported to be "wobbling" when it was closed.

The broadcast media reported that thunderstorm winds had downed a billboard and power lines near the intersection of Himes Avenue and West Columbus Drive. Falling tree branches brought down more power lines down about one mile to the north on North Grady Avenue, causing 200 homes to lose power.
A trained weather spotter reported two inches of rain in 30 minutes that caused street flooding near the intersection of Himes Avenue and West Columbus Drive.
Lightning struck and killed a 23 year old man while he was playing soccer at Billy Bowlegs Park in Fort Myers.
A funnel cloud was seen over the Palm Beach Zoo at Dreher Park.
Nickel-size hail was seen.
Roof blown off house and large trees down along State Road 21.  Report by TV 20 in Gainesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: 1459:  Storm spotter reported 18" of water on the side of roads in downtown Old St. Augustine. 1510:  Numerous reports of flooded streets which had to be closed because they were impassable.1550:  Significant flooding was reported along State Road 16 and Masters Drive.~1600:  Water was reported in 3 homes throughout St. Johns County.1600:  Water was reported in a home at 230 Segovia Road.
Lightning stuck a man at the Town and Country Motel in Vilano Beach.  His injuries were reported as 'not serious.'

News4Jax website reported that police had to close King Street from U.S. 1 to Malaga Street due to flooding and San Carlos Ave. from U.S. 1 to San Marco Ave. due to a downed power line in St. Augustine.
Nickel size hail was reported near I-95 and Martin Luther King Blvd in downtown Jacksonville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Myakka River at Myakka Head was in flood from June 21 until July 10th with a crest of 12.46 feet on June 24th. Flood stage is 7 feet.
The Horse Creek near Arcadia was in flood from June 22nd until July 1 with a crest of 19.71 fee on June 22nd. Flood stage is 12 feet.
The Peace River at Arcadia was in flood from June 22nd to July 4 with a crest of 15.41 feet on June 26th. The flood stage is 11 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: 10 homes were flooded by storm waters near Moncrief Village, Lenox Circle and West 45th Street.
Doppler Radar indicated 3.5 inches of rain fell within a few hours near West Leonard Circle and West 33rd Street in downtown Jacksonville.
Hourly rainfall rate of 4 inches (from 1600-1700) near U.S. Highway 1 and Edgewood Ave. on the Jacksonville Northside.
A worker was stuck by lightning at the Turtle Shores subdivision in North Vilano Beach.  He was still conscious after the strike and life-flight was not needed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed trees and limbs which downed power lines in Zephyrhills. Two businesses on Main Street had their roofs peeled back and water poured in, soaking customers at a restaurant and damaging leather at a saddle company. A Cessna 182 airplane was flipped over on its back and many other owners reported minor damage to their aircraft.
A 1.3 million dollar home just off Enterprise Road in Safety Harbor was struck by lightning. The resulting two alarm fire took 50 firefighters from six departments to put out.
Lightning struck a home on Bay Hills Boulevard and ignited a blaze in the attic.

Sparks were flying out of a power panel in the garage of a Sea Pines Circle home in Countryside after lightning struck the home. The fire in the panel was put out with an fire extinguisher but the fire had already spread into the walls of the home. Eighteen fire trucks responded to the fire but the house was considered a total loss.

Heavy rainfall from Tropical Storm Bill caused many streets in the south part of the county to flood.  Rainfall totals of four to seven inches were estimated in a six hour period.  The flooding continued into the  morning hours with many streets closed for several hours.

Heavy rainfall from Tropical Storm Bill caused many streets in the south part of the county to flood.  Rainfall totals of four to seven inches were estimated in a six hour period.  The flooding continued into the  morning hours with many streets closed for several hours.
A man inside a shed at a nursery was shocked when lightning entered through a telephone.  He suffered memory loss and was transported to a local hospital.
A funnel cloud was reported near I-75 south of Exit 17.
A SKYWARN spotter saw three-quarter inch hail.

Most of the flooding problems occurred east of Highway 19 from Cardinal Lane to Green Acres and in the Crystal Manor area. Several homes along South Line Road in Lecanto had 18 inches of water inside after a county drainage retention pond overflowed. Rainfall totals for the day were 10.53 inches 3 miles east of Floral City and 6.51 inches in Chassahowitzka.
The Sheriff and FHP reported three-quarter inch hail covering the roadway at the intersection of I-75 and CR 951.
trees and power lines down near Sparr.  Approximately 15 trees down on road between Sparr and Fort McCoy including one down on a barn.  Trees down in Fort McCoy.
61 mph wind measured at TV 4 WJXT Studios.
Power lines down on Albert Street and near Tallyrand Docks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skywarn Spotter reported a funnel cloud over the Saint Johns River in downtown Jacksonville, between the Matthews Bridge and Metro Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cypress Creek at Worthington Gardens was in flood from June 22nd until July 8th with a crest of 9.2 feet on June 27th. The flood stage is 8 feet.
Nickel to quarter size hail was reported from several sources in the area.
Trees and power lines down at St. Johns Power Park.  Winds estimated by Jacksonville Electric Authority (JEA) employees at 65 to 70 mph.
The Manatee River at Myakka Head experienced a record crest of 20.85 feet on June 22nd. The flood stage is 11 feet.
Lightning caused a fire that damaged the roof, attic, and laundry area of eight unit apartment complex.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist tropical air mass lead to widespread thunderstorms and rain across the west central coast of Florida. Numerous severe weather events occurred across northern Hillsborough County where radar detected two thunderstorms that collided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees, limbs, and power lines were down in the Lake Magdalene and New Tampa Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist tropical air mass lead to widespread thunderstorms and rain across the west central coast of Florida. Numerous severe weather events occurred across northern Hillsborough County where radar detected two thunderstorms that collided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator reported one inch hail at the intersection of Fowler and 30th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist tropical air mass lead to widespread thunderstorms and rain across the west central coast of Florida. Numerous severe weather events occurred across northern Hillsborough County where radar detected two thunderstorms that collided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were down in the Citrus Park area due to fallen trees and limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist tropical air mass lead to widespread thunderstorms and rain across the west central coast of Florida. Numerous severe weather events occurred across northern Hillsborough County where radar detected two thunderstorms that collided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three large oak trees were down in a yard in Carrollwood.
Nickel size hail was reported by the media near 66th St and Bryan Dairy Road.
Thunderstorm winds pushed over a gas station roof covering the pumps near 49th Street and 110th Avenue and fallen trees brought down power lines just to the west on 52nd Street.

The thunderstorm struck near Hillsborough Avenue and Town N Country Boulevard toppling two fireworks tents and the rain ruined all of their products. Two people in one of the tents received minor injuries of cuts and scrapes during the event. A utility company reported numerous tree limbs and power lines down from Town N County to three miles north of Town N Country.

The tornado occurred on the north end of a large thunderstorm causing mostly tree damage in rural Hillsborough County.  The tornado started at the end of Collins Ranch Road and traveled north across Thonotosassa road at the County Park and Boat Ramp. It then continued north into the southeast corner of Lake Thonotosassa where it because stationary and "sucked up boat loads of water" according to one observer. Finally the tornado came back onshore and headed north just west of Hendry Ranch Road before dissipating a short time later. Most of the damage from the tornado occurred at the end of Collins Ranch Road where several light structures were destroyed and trees were snapped off at the base. One was thrown onto a nearby vehicle.  Boaters on Lake Thonotosassa were stuck in the middle of the lake as they watched the tornado plow through the boat ramp area.  Observers witnessed trees with limbs reaching toward the sky before the entire tree was lifted into the air.
A National Weather Service storm survey found three 50 foot pines snapped off at the 20 foot level and hundreds of limbs down.
A wood frame home under construction was blown down.

Thunderstorm wind gusts to 63 MPH were measured by an amateur radio weather station. Coincidently, an amateur radio Field Day site testing emergency communications reported damage to antennas, awnings, and tents.
Lightning strike destroyed a 1,200 square foot section of a decorative outer brick wall on the back edge of Buddy Taylor Middle School, near the gymnasium.  Bricks fell on a school walkway.
Lightning struck a home on Ames Way and destroyed a power meter box while another strike on Tallahassee Drive put a hole in the roof of a home.
Thunderstorm downburst winds toppled trees with diameters of two feet onto four homes near the intersection of Rockcrusher Road and Vendable Road. One home sustained moderate damage to the roof.
Lightning entered a home through the cable TV or phone line and destroyed the entertainment center and phone. An energy surge also tripped every circuit breaker and damaged the water heater.
Thunderstorm winds downed trees and blew the roof off a mobile home.

Lightning started a mobile home on fire and the home burned to the ground.  A man was sleeping inside at the time but an alert neighbor pounded on the walls and doors until the man exited the burning home.

Spyglass Apartments, 700 SW 62nd Blvd, DD Building.  Fire sparked by lightning strike burned roof and attic. Two apartment units received the bulk of the damage due to smoke and water damage.  Building was insured, however resident losses were not.

Thunderstorms moved in from the Gulf of Mexico over an extended period of time causing a waterspout  four miles west of Longboat Pass and a brief tornado in Palmetto. The tornado damage consisted mostly of car port damage in a mobile home park near 33rd St W and 8th Ave W, and roof damage to a canning factory on the 3600 block of U.S. Highway 41 North.  Visual confirmation of the tornado was obtained from a Captain of the local fire district.
A local utility company reported numerous trees and power lines reported down.
Golfball size hail was reported by Emergency Management.
A local utility reported numerous trees and power lines down across the city of Venice.

State Road 490 in the western part of the county was closed due to flooding and motorists were stranded in the streets in Crystal River. A gully overflowed near Highway 19 South in Homosassa and the water flowed into a nearby restaurant "like a river". Numerous cars received moderate damage as the flood waters inundated an auto dealer lot. Rain fall totals for the day were 4.51 inches in Chassahowitzka and 3.36 inches 3 miles east of Floral City.
Trees and power lines were downed by a microburst, blocking Wiggins road near the intersection of Kirkland. The microburst traveled southeast to the Lakeland Linder Regional Airport in Polk county.
Golf-ball size hail was seen near SW 136 Street and Krome Avenue.
The microburst induced thunderstorm winds started in Plant City and passed through the airport kicking up light debris but did not cause reportable damage.
Thunderstorm winds knocked down several trees and numerous branches. One tree fell onto a home on the 400 block of Honeybee Lane.  At least one car port and one metal shed were destroyed.
Lightning struck an apartment building in South Tampa causing a fire in the attic and an evacuation of the building.

Thunderstorm winds downed trees which took down power lines on Mariner Blvd. and the intersection of Sunshine Grove and State Highway 50. Elsewhere in the county, the Brooksville airport recorded 46 knot winds at 254 PM EDT.
Thunderstorm winds took the roof off of a mobile home, destroyed a car port, and pushed trees onto at least two vehicles.
The Withlacoochee River at Dunnellon briefly reached flood stage of 29 feet.

A mobile home park near Highway 41 and Croom Road in Brooksville experienced a water rise of six feet in a few hours. This resulted in flooded homes and vehicles. One indirect fatality was attributed to the heavy rain as a car hydroplaned and crashed near Highway 19 and Northcliffe in Spring Hill.  Masaryktown received 5.05 inches of rain during the day, 3 miles northeast of Spring Hill received 4.30 inches, and Brooksville received 4.09 inches.

Heavy rains caused flooding that impacted 125 properties and flooded 25 homes and businesses in the Hudson, Odessa, Wesley Chapel, and Zephyrhills area. Nine roads were damaged to a total of $150,000. The rainfall totals for the day were 8.01 inches 6 miles east northeast of Tarpon Springs, 6.36 inches in Gowers Corner, and 5.65 inches in Hudson.
Heavy rains turned the low lying areas of River Drive in Valrico into a canoe trail. Rain fall totals for the day were 4.25 inches in Lutz and 3.84 inches 4 miles east of Citrus Park.
Two large oak trees and numerous branches were downed by thunderstorm winds and blocked Oak Lane Road near the intersection of Bloomingdale and Lithia Pinecrest.

Strong thunderstorms moved into Bradenton Beach from the Gulf of Mexico and produced widespread damage to trees and vegetation.  About $15,000 in damage occurred at a two story motel along the coast when the roof was blown off allowing rain to pour into three units on the top floor. No one was injured at the motel but 20 people were relocated across the street to another motel. The storm also caused damage to the roof of a pump house and two concrete block walls collapsed at a construction site.
Fire and Rescue reported numerous trees down on the island after the thunderstorm passed.
Thunderstorm winds pushed down trees which brought down power lines.

The Alafia River at Lithia crested four and a half feet above flood stage at 17.56 feet on June 23rd. Several homes in the River Drive area of Valrico had to evacuate.  Rainfall totals during the previous five days included 9.67 inches in Ruskin, 8.89 inches in Odessa, and 6.83 inches in Tampa.
The Little Manatee River at Wimauma crested on June 22nd at 17.67 feet.  The flood stage is 11 feet.
Rain fall totals for the day were 4.80 inches at Pinellas Point and 4.05 inches in Largo. The saturated grounds finally gave way to the heavy rain leading to flooded roads and vehicles.
The Peace River near Bartow crested at 8.32 feet on June 25th. The flood stage is 8 feet.

Lightning struck the Walter Pownell Service Center.  The computer system was damaged and an employee was injured when he received a shock from the computer he was working at and was thrown across the room. The amount of damage was estimated.

A large tree fell onto the roof of a home near the intersection of 24th Avenue and 80th Street. Damage to car ports and other light damage occurred at a  mobile home park near 1st Avenue and 66th Street.
A trained storm spotter reported large hail and damaging wind near the intersection of U.S. Highway 41 and Corkscrew Road.
A lightning strike sparked a fire that caused moderate damage to a house on Cottonwood Drive.
Thunderstorm winds damaged a pool cage and part of a roof.

A single rotating thunderstorm moved north through Charlotte Harbor and spawned a brief tornado in the city of Charlotte Harbor. Damage consisted mostly of downed trees and power lines.  However, a gas station awning was blown down and a roof was damaged at a blood bank under construction.  The damage was limited to areas where Kingsway, Edgewater, and Harborview cross U.S. Highway 41.

A tornado passed through Dade City and damaged mostly trees and power lines over an area 12 blocks long and one block wide, basically along Coleman Street between 7th Ave and 17th Ave. The tornado wind estimate was 70 mph.  Damage to four homes and three cars were a result of trees falling on them. Many trees were pushed over by the tornado but most of these trees had signs of rot and the ground was saturated. The local utility reported 2,700 people without power after the event. Damage was estimated at $40,000 but cleanup costs was estimated at $60,000.

Three road construction workers were struck by lightning at the corner of U.S. 19 and Drew Street while standing under a large oak tree. All three were transported to a hospital and complained about internal pain, uncontrollable twitching, and numbness.
Emergency Management reported penny size hail covering the ground on Chancey Road and Tucker Road.
Law enforcement reported several large branches were downed by thunderstorm winds and were blocking Bloomingdale Avenue.

Thunderstorm winds damaged several car ports at the Jamaica Bay Mobile Home Park. Other damage included torn screens in lanai areas, a pushed over wood fence in disrepair, and loose soffets blown down.
Quarter-size hail fell near the intersection of the Beeline Expressway and SR 786.
A pilot saw a brief tornado touchdown in the everglades south of  I-75.
Lightning struck a tree and the strike traveled through the trees root system and burned a hole through a plastic natural gas line.  The fire was extinguished quickly after the gas was turned off.
High winds from a thunderstorm blew down several trees near Barrineau Park.
A 50 year old male in the Pinellas Bay Way Channel was injured when lightning struck his boat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 54 year old male was stuck on Andrew Robinson Dr.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved ashore from the Atlantic near the Town of Palm Beach and continued west.  The tornado did very minor damage to a construction site.

A lightning strike at the Grassy Pointe subdivision produced two minor injuries to plumbers working at a vacant log on Denise Street. They complained about a "tingly feeling" and were checked out by paramedics and released.
Lightning struck a mobile home that was being refurbished near Zolfo Springs. The resulting fire destroyed the home and caused damage to three other mobile homes nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked a fire that destroyed a mobile home. A woman inside the mobile home suffered smoke inhalation and a dog inside  perished.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused extensive street flooding along U.S. Highway 17/Park Ave in Orange park and south to near the Eagle Harbor Subdivision.  Most significant flooding at Park Ave and Kingsley Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported seeing a funnel cloud around Oak Run near the County Road 484 and State Road 200 intersection.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from the remains of Tropical Storm Bill cause several roads in the south part of the county to flood and become impassable for several hours.  Rainfall totals from the storm across the area were eight to ten inches.

Heavy rainfall from Tropical Storm Bill continued from June 30 over most of the county.  Several roads had standing water in them and they were impassable for several hours.  Rainfall totals from the storm across the area were seven to eight inches.

A waterspout moved ashore from the Atlantic near 75th Street and Collins Avenue on Miami Beach.  The tornado overturned a Beach Patrol lifeguard stand and uprooted a sea grape tree which damaged a sidewalk and patio.    The tornado then dissipated.

Cypress Creek at Worthington Gardens crested at 10.27 feet at 10:45 AM EST on the 13th. Flood stage is 8 feet. Damage continued to occur to area roads but no significant damage was reported to homes or businesses.

A father and son were fishing from a pier near I-10 and Escambia Bay.  Lightning struck a tall pine tree 20 feet away.  The lightning ran down the tree, up the pier where the men were fishing and struck both of them.  The 42 year old son died at the scene.  His 69 year old father was taken to the hospital and kept for a couple of days and released.

A weak tornado touched down near Carlton Cemetery Road, just south of the Perry-Foley Airport where a barn was heavily damaged.  Intermittent damage occurred throughout neighborhoods along Foley Cut-Off Road which were littered by uprooted trees and downed power lines.  Several homes sustained minor damage.  One mobile was damaged by a fallen live oak tree.  The tornado dissipated just northeast of County Road 356 and Faulkner Road.  Reported by the Taylor County EMA.
High winds from a thunderstorm blew over a brick wall at a business.  No injuries were reported.
High winds from thunderstorms blew a roof off a shed and also blew down several trees near Allentown.

Heavy rainfall from Tropical Storm Bill continued from June 30 over the south portion of the county.  Many roads in the south part of the county were flooded and impassable for several hours.  Rainfall totals from the storm across the area were eight to nine inches.
Thunderstorm winds blew down trees in the Hobe Sound and Stuart areas.
The public reported a funnel cloud over Port St. Lucie.

Heavy rains from a nearly stationary line of thunderstorms caused flooding between Baker and Dorcas.  Several roads had to be closed across this area.  A bridge near Dorcas was partially washed out by the high water.  Five to six inches of rainfall fell across this area in about an hour.
High winds from a thunderstorm blew down several trees around Niceville.

3 to 5 inches of rain with localized street and poor drainage area flooding.  Street flooding most pronounced in the Hammock area. Florida Park Drive experienced street flooding.  Estimated rain totals:Emergency Operating Center: 1.95 in.Indian Trails REACT Base Six: 3.5 in.Palm Harbor North AF2C: 1.5 in.Cypress Knoll WB2CIC - 3.00 In.Seminole Woods REACT Thirty-six: 5.00 in.Pine Lakes: 4.75 In.

Numerous damage reports:75 Federal Lane - House with awning ripped off, some roof damage.68 Pitman Lane - Downed tree damaged a truck.40 Burbank - Downed tree fell through the roof of a house.Palm Coast Parkway and Parkview Drive - Traffic signal downed and in roadway.Cypress Point Parkway - Trees down on roadway.211 Birchwood - Tree down on house.Seven Wonders and Seven Champions - Tree down across intersection.

Street and poor drainage flooding occurred at the intersection of Fort Caroline Rd. and Monument Rd.  Surface observations and radar estimated rainfall rates indicate hourly accumulations of 2 inches per hour in this area.

A thirty-two year old male from Georgia drowned while swimming in rough surf  at an unguarded beach east of Casio Beach.  The male went swimming with a friend when he got into trouble in the rough surf.  The friend was able to make it ashore but the man drowned.  Red flags warning of dangerous surf were flying at the time.

A forty-six year old male from Tennessee disappeared while swimming in rough surf.  His body did not wash ashore until the next day.  The male was swimming in rough surf in an unguarded area east of Casino Beach.

A thirty year old male from Texas drowned while swimming in rough surf east of Casino Beach.  He was swimming in an unguarded part of the beach when he disappeared.  Life guards said they had to make numerous rescues from the rough surf on Pensacola Beach that weekend.

An eighteen year old resident alien from Mexico disappeared in rough surf near Fort Pickens.  He worked for a landscaping company who were treating their employees to a day at the beach.  He drowned in the rough surf.
Lightning struck a house and started a fire.  The fire was extinguished by the fire department but not before the house suffered damage.  No one was injured.
A SKYWARN spotter saw a funnel cloud near U.S. 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree in a pasture killing 22 nearby pregnant cows. Owners reported each cow was worth at least $2500.

Very heavy rainfall fell across southwest Florida with radar estimated amounts of 8 to 10 inches.  Naples measured a record 6.99 inches.  The resulting flood closed numerous roads in Collier County and washed out a road in Glades County. Numerous cars were stalled.  Houses and businesses including a shopping mall suffered minor flooding damage.

Very heavy rainfall fell across southwest Florida with radar estimated amounts of 8 to 10 inches.  Naples measured a record 6.99 inches.  The resulting flood closed numerous roads in Collier County and washed out a road in Glades County. Numerous cars were stalled.  Houses and businesses including a shopping mall suffered minor flooding damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported a funnel cloud over southern Biscayne Bay.

Swells from hurricane Isabel, that was passing about 1000 miles to the east of southeast Florida, capsized a watercraft at the Boynton Beach Inlet injuring two men.  A 14-year-old swimmer was rescued from high waves near Juno Beach Pier.  Minor beach erosion was experienced.
Fire-Rescue reported a funnel cloud over Hallandale Beach Boulevard.
Law enforcement officers reported a funnel cloud just west of Wabasso.
An off duty National Weather Service meteorologist playing golf on the Palmetto Golf Course, saw a tornado form near the Metro Zoo and move toward the east northeast.  No damage was observed.
A SKYWARN spotter saw a funnel cloud near I-95 and Yamato Road.

Lightning struck a 45-foot sail boat on its way to Bimini.  The lightning strike caused a fire that completely destroyed the craft.  The only occupant on board was forced into the water where he suffered minor injuries before being rescued by the Coast Guard.
A SKYWARN spotter sighted a funnel cloud just southwest of Lake Worth.
Two residents drowned in rip currents at an unguarded section of beach.  Both persons were weak swimmers and were in 4.5 feet of water when the rip currents pulled them into deeper water.
A man drowned in a rip current at Fort Lauderdale Beach.

A Georgia man was on a personal watercraft when he was struck by lightning.  He was on his way  to shore to beat approaching thunderstorms when he was struck.  He was transported and treated at the hospital and was released after a couple of hours.
Fire started by lightning destroyed a mobile home on Maytown Road.
Large trees down in Faver Dykes State Park.
Thunderstorm winds damaged the roofs of two houses and blew down trees in Port St. Lucie.
Thunderstorms winds swept across the town of Okeechobee damaging 3 roofs, many fences and some screen porches.
Local law enforcement officials reported a funnel cloud southwest of Port St. Lucie.

A fifty seven year old Missouri man was swimming in rough surf near James Lee Park when he started having trouble staying afloat.  He was pulled out of the water but later died at a local hospital.  Red flags were flying at the time of the drowning.
High winds blew part of the metal siding off a county building that was used to house large equipment..

A sixty six year old male from the local area jumped in the gulf to rescue a kid who was having trouble in the rough water.  The youngster was brought ashore and was okay.  The male drowned in the rough surf.  Yellow flags were flying along the beach at the time of the drowning.
A spotter observed a funnel cloud for about five minutes near Duke Field.  The funnel cloud dissipated and never touched down.
Thunderstorm winds blew down trees in Indian Harbor Beach.
Near 3 inches of rain produced widespread street flooding in Kissimmee.
Numerous trees down across County Roads 229 and 235 near the Union-Bradford County line.
Damage reported by Union County Sheriffs Officer transiting western Bradford County along County Roads 229 and 235.  Numerous large trees down on roadways near Bradford-Union County Line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Electric company reported numerous tree tops were snapped off and fell over power lines between La Crosse and Brooker in northern Alachua county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Waterspout moved ashore and crossed Key Haven Road in Key Haven section of Key West.  Palm fronds were broken off palm trees, but there was no damage to structures or boats.
Local fire department personnel reported two funnel clouds southwest of Palm Bay.
Thunderstorm winds damaged the roof and windows of a house northwest of Okeechobee.
A chronic slow rise in ditch and pond water levels over the previous weeks culminated with about four inches of rain and the flooding of 35 homes in the Kissimmee and St. Cloud communities.
Fire from a lightning strike destroyed a church in Deltona.  Damage estimates were near $250,000.
An 81-year-old man was seriously injured by a lightning strike as he walked down the sidewalk near Johnson Street
CBS-4 measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph at their T.V studio.

A thirty one year old female from Indiana jumped into the gulf to rescue her son who was having trouble swimming in the rough surf.  Both of them were pulled out of the rough water and transported to a local hospital.  She was pronounced dead and her son was treated and released.  The drowning occurred around James Lee Park where red flags warning of dangerous surf were flying.  The drownings in Okaloosa county were part of a rash of drownings that occurred along the beaches of Walton and Okaloosa counties on this date.  Six people drowned in Walton county that day.  The rough water was the result of swells of four to six feet moving ashore.  Ten people along the Okaloosa county beaches had to be transported to local hospitals where they were treated and released.  Many others had to be pulled from the rough surf where they were treated on the beach .  Officials were flying and driving along the beaches to warn people of the dangerous surf.  People would exit the water and then go back in after the officials had left.

A 49 year old male, from the local area, was surfing in the waters of the Gulf of Mexico in front of the Eglin Community Beach Club near Destin when he was struck by a bolt of lightning.   Other surfers pulled the man out of the water, and emergency personnel transported him to a local hospital, where he later died.  Thunderstorms had moved through the area earlier in the morning.  The overcast sky, after most of the storms had moved out of the area, produced the isolated, but deadly, lightning bolt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: State Warning Point relayed that a mobile home roof was blown off and some trees were down at a public residence.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: 23 year old foreign exchange student from India pulled into the surf around 3 pm EDT. WFO Jacksonville Surf Forecast indicated a "Moderate Risk of Rip Currents."  Source of report St. Augustine Record.
A 35 year old construction worker was struck by lightning while in his truck. CPR was performed then he was taken to a hospital where he was in critical but stable condition.
A thunderstorm downburst wind produced minor to moderate damage to eight manufactured homes.
Thunderstorm winds down trees and took the roof off of one mobile home in Bushnell.
A trained SKYWARN Spotter reported nickel size hail at the intersection of North Tampa Street and West Columbus Drive.
Near interstate 95 ans U.S. Highway 1 interchange in southern St. Johns County.
Two funnel clouds sighted near Palm Coast.
Heavy rainfall occurred with a line of thunderstorms causing street flooding in poor drainage areas.  A car was stalled in street flooding on U.S. 17 and Stiles Ave.
Billboard and numerous power lines down at the Interstate 95 and U.S. Highway 1 interchange in southern St. Johns County.
The lightning came through the phone line at the office but the person felt fine and went home. Later that evening the person felt sick and called 911 and was transported to the hospital.

Lightning struck a tree adjacent to a Dune Allen Beach house.  The bolt bounced through the house, blowing doors off their hinges and knocking cabinets off the walls.  One occupant was slightly injured when she was thrown into a kitchen wall by the force of the explosion.  Reported by the Destin Log.
